identity.title: Alumina Refining and Primary Aluminum Production

lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. businesses repeatedly refining alumina from bauxite or producing primary aluminum and aluminum alloys from alumina for sale to external customers, including integrated primary forming where it is part of the same operating business.
lens.excluded_activities: Bauxite mining alone, secondary aluminum and scrap remelting, downstream processors using purchased aluminum, captive or idle assets without external recurring revenue, passive entities, and operations without transferable control.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: High-volume sales of alumina, primary aluminum, billet, slab, ingot, and related alloy forms to smelters, rolling mills, extruders, foundries, fabricators, and industrial customers, priced from commodity benchmarks, regional and product premiums, alloy and shape specifications, energy economics, freight, tariffs, and contractual terms.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader 331300 aluminum-production and processing workforce, in which production occupations are 51.25% of employment and maintenance, material-moving, technical, commercial, and administrative roles also matter. NIST identifies predictive maintenance, quality control, energy management, forecasting, scheduling, and digital twins as manufacturing AI uses. I translate that broader occupational and use-case evidence into wage-weighted remaining exposure for primary production. The bridge may overstate opportunity because primary refineries and smelters already use extensive continuous-process control and understate it where energy optimization and cell analytics are unusually valuable.
a.rationale: AI can improve cell and refinery monitoring, anomaly and defect detection, predictive maintenance, energy and raw-material optimization, laboratory workflows, production planning, procurement, logistics, sales support, and administration. Furnace, potline, materials, maintenance, environmental, safety, and emergency work remains physical and accountable.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-wage study for operating U.S. alumina refineries and primary smelters separating existing process automation from remaining human work, with hours and compensation by task, would materially change the range.
caveats: OEWS combines primary production with downstream aluminum processing.
Manufacturing AI examples establish capability, not adoption or incremental labor displacement at U.S. smelters.

rho.rationale: Continuous, sensor-rich refinery and smelter processes, historians, quality measurements, and very high energy costs support implementation. Legacy controls, sparse failure labels, extreme equipment conditions, cyber-physical risk, safety and environmental constraints, process interdependence, and the enormous cost of false actions constrain autonomous deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level deployments reporting commissioning cost, prediction accuracy, operator overrides, energy or yield improvement, maintenance avoidance, downtime, safety incidents, and audited labor hours saved would narrow the estimate.
caveats: Energy, yield, and uptime improvements do not necessarily substitute labor.
Alumina refineries, modern smelters, older potlines, and integrated casthouses have different data and control maturity.

e.rationale: Census reports only 32 employer establishments, while economically viable alumina refineries and primary smelters are generally enormous, capital-intensive strategic assets owned by large or integrated groups. A frozen count of four derived with a Machinery EBITDA margin is therefore likely misleading for the $1 million to $10 million EBITDA lens. A small chance remains for an independent specialty, alloy, or primary-form operation that is classified here and fits the band.
e.change_evidence: A named review of all 32 establishments covering operating status, ultimate ownership, external revenue, primary versus secondary feedstock, normalized EBITDA, energy contracts, environmental liabilities, and transferable control would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: Establishments are not firms and include units of large corporate groups.
The frozen n is margin-bridged from Machinery, an especially poor analogue for energy- and capital-intensive primary aluminum.

s5.bridge: SBA reports that 47% of family-business owners expecting retirement within five years lack a successor. I use that only as broad evidence of succession pressure, then apply a large haircut because this industry is dominated by corporate, integrated, joint-venture, or strategic infrastructure assets rather than conventional family businesses. Even for a rare eligible operator, energy contracts, environmental obligations, capital needs, customer qualification, and government scrutiny can prevent a qualifying control transfer.
s5.rationale: A rare independent eligible producer could transact because of succession, restructuring, or capital needs, but closures, recapitalizations, strategic mergers, asset sales, and joint-venture changes are more plausible than ordinary LMM control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of U.S. refinery, smelter, and primary-alloy transactions matched to eligible firms, including ownership, transaction form, operating status, and EBITDA, would replace the proxy.
caveats: The SBA statistic is cross-industry and is not a completed-transfer rate.
Plant or asset sales, closures, and changes in joint-venture interests may not qualify as transfers of eligible firms.

q.bridge: Alcoa describes primary aluminum pricing as exposed to transparent LME benchmarks, regional and product premiums, global supply and demand, inventories, transportation, tariffs, shape, and alloy. I translate those large-company disclosures to an eligible LMM producer. Benchmark transparency and trade exposure likely compress retention, while proprietary energy, yield, cell-life, reliability, alloy, or service gains may remain private for a time.
q.rationale: Customers and competitors can observe benchmark metal prices, premiums, and broad energy and logistics conditions, encouraging pass-through. Process stability, lower energy intensity, improved yield, fewer outages, differentiated alloys, qualification, and dependable delivery protect a minority of gains.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contracts, premium and surcharge histories, bid data, customer renegotiations, and gross-margin persistence after verified AI or automation projects would materially change the range.
caveats: Alumina, commodity primary metal, value-added alloys, and casthouse products have different pricing power.
Alcoa is much larger and more vertically integrated than the target lens.

d5.bridge: BLS projects broader 331300 real output from $38.1 billion in 2024 to $39.9 billion in 2034, about 0.5% annually. USGS reports substantial U.S. secondary aluminum recovery alongside primary production, while Alcoa describes demand across transportation, packaging, building, electrical, industrial, and other markets. I use these as bounds for a five-year U.S. physical-demand ratio. Broader real output includes downstream processing and prices, and end-use aluminum demand does not map one-for-one to domestic primary output because imports and recycled metal can substitute.
d5.rationale: Electrification, grid investment, lightweight transportation, aerospace, packaging, construction, and industrial demand support aluminum use. Recycling, imports, domestic curtailments, power costs, substitution, trade policy, and cyclicality can leave U.S. primary production flat or declining despite growing aluminum consumption.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of U.S. alumina and primary aluminum shipments in physical tons, separated from secondary metal and imports and tied to announced restarts, closures, power contracts, and capacity projects, would materially improve the estimate.
caveats: BLS output covers downstream aluminum processing as well as NAICS 331313 activities.
USGS industry totals do not isolate the eligible LMM lens, and recycled metal can satisfy end demand without domestic primary output.

o.rationale: Customers require a physical, permitted operator to refine alumina or run electrolysis, casting, alloying, quality assurance, environmental controls, safety systems, and delivery. AI can automate internal tasks but cannot replace the accountable producer within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of rapid customer insourcing, autonomous permitted plants without accountable operating organizations, or widespread material substitution eliminating primary aluminum demand would lower the estimate.
caveats: Imports or recycled aluminum can displace a domestic operator without making software the supplier.
Operator requirement does not imply unchanged headcount or ownership.

narrative.executive_view: Primary aluminum is a sensor-rich, energy-intensive continuous industry where AI can improve process stability, energy use, maintenance, quality, and planning. The acquisition lens is the central weakness: the already-low frozen count is likely overstated because ordinary LMM firms rarely own viable refineries or smelters.
narrative.ai_impact: Models can detect cell and equipment anomalies, predict failures, optimize energy and material inputs, support laboratory and quality decisions, and improve scheduling, logistics, procurement, sales, and administrative work. Hazardous physical operations, maintenance, permitting, environmental controls, and responsible release remain.
narrative.value_capture: Energy, yield, reliability, alloy differentiation, qualification, and delivery can protect some gains. Transparent LME pricing, observable premiums, global capacity, imports, tariffs, and customer bargaining pass much of the benefit through.
narrative.firm_availability: Only 32 employer establishments are reported, and most viable assets are likely large-group operations. Machinery-margin bridging is especially unreliable here, so few if any businesses may satisfy independent external revenue, $1 million to $10 million EBITDA, and transferable-control requirements.
narrative.demand_durability: Aluminum serves transportation, packaging, construction, electrical, aerospace, and industrial markets, but growing use can be supplied by imports and recycled metal rather than domestic primary production. Nearly all remaining primary volume still requires an accountable physical operator.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are energy prices and contracts, curtailment or closure, commodity and premium volatility, imports and tariffs, recycled-metal substitution, environmental liabilities, catastrophic process errors, legacy integration, cyber-physical security, capital intensity, and severe LMM misclassification.
narrative.principal_driver: High energy and downtime costs make reliable AI improvements in process control, maintenance, yield, and planning economically meaningful.
narrative.principal_weakness: The LMM acquisition universe may be effectively empty because viable primary-aluminum assets are capital-intensive and concentrated in large or strategic owners.

sources.title: 331313: Alumina Refining and Primary Aluminum Production - Census Bureau Profile
sources.supports: Defines the industry and reports 32 employer establishments in the 2023 County Business Patterns profile.

sources.title: Alumina and Aluminum Production and Processing - May 2023 OEWS
sources.supports: Provides the broader 331300 occupational mix, including a 51.25% production-occupation share and employment across maintenance, materials, technical, management, commercial, and office roles.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing
sources.supports: Documents manufacturing AI applications in predictive maintenance, quality, demand forecasting, energy and resource management, scheduling, and digital twins, plus adoption barriers.

sources.title: Alcoa Corporation 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Documents vertical integration, primary-aluminum end markets, LME and premium pricing, supply-demand and inventory exposure, tariffs, transportation, energy, capacity, and operating risks.

sources.title: Mineral Commodity Summaries 2026 - Aluminum
sources.supports: Provides U.S. primary aluminum value and industry statistics and reports about 3.6 million tons of aluminum recovered from purchased scrap in 2025, supporting primary-versus-secondary substitution analysis.

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry
sources.supports: Projects broader 331300 real output from $38.1 billion in 2024 to $39.9 billion in 2034, approximately 0.5% annually.

sources.title: 5 Challenges for Family-Owned Businesses
sources.supports: Reports that 47% of owners expecting to retire within five years do not have a successor, used only as a broad succession-pressure proxy.
